title: Who really owns the shared courtyard?
summary: Argue who has the strongest claim to a residential courtyard shared by many households — the legal owners, the daily users, or no one.
topic: Who has the strongest claim to a shared residential courtyard
prompt: Write a 450–600-word essay arguing who really 'owns' a courtyard shared by the residents of an apartment block — the people on the deeds, the people who use it daily, or no one in particular. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, passive voice, and hedging.
hintStarters: What a courtyard like this really tests is not the law of property but…
Granted, the residents whose names appear on the deeds can reasonably claim that…
It is the grandmother who waters the plants every morning, rather than the absent landlord, who…
The question is not who paid for the ground but who…

hintVocab.meaningVi: giấy chứng nhận quyền sở hữu
quyền sở hữu trên thực tế
bi kịch của tài sản chung
có dấu vết của đời sống thường nhật

sampleText:
Behind a row of flats in Lisbon there is a small courtyard, paved with cracked tiles and lined with potted lemon trees. On paper it belongs to a property company whose office is three districts away. In practice it belongs to whoever happens to be sitting on the worn bench at six in the evening. The distance between those two facts is where the real question lives.

What a courtyard like this really tests is not the law of property but the difference between holding a title and exercising care. A title deed records who paid; it says nothing about who waters the lemon trees, sweeps the leaves after a storm, or warns the children away from the broken drain. In this courtyard, that work is done by an eighty-year-old woman named Dona Inês, who has never owned a square metre of it. It is she, rather than the absentee company, who has made the space liveable. To say she has no claim because her name is missing from a document seems to mistake the deed for the place.

A second example sharpens the point. When the company recently proposed fencing off half the courtyard to rent as parking, it was not the law that stopped them but the residents, who simply kept using the space as they always had. Plant pots were not moved. Chairs were not removed. The fence was quietly abandoned. What this reveals is that de facto ownership — ownership through daily use and stewardship — can be more durable than the kind written into a contract.

Granted, the residents whose company holds the deeds can reasonably claim that property without legal protection is no property at all. If anyone who lingers in a space could lay claim to it, no owner would ever feel secure, and few would invest in maintaining anything. The objection is not trivial: stewardship that rests on goodwill alone tends to collapse the moment goodwill runs out, and someone has to be answerable when the drain finally bursts.

Even so, the case for daily users is not that they should hold the deeds, but that ownership of a shared space is better understood as a relationship than as a fact. The company's claim is real; what it is missing is the relationship. A courtyard that is owned but never visited is, in any meaningful sense, abandoned, while one that is used, tended, and defended every day belongs — morally, if not legally — to the people who keep it alive.

It is precisely because shared spaces resist single ownership that they so often fall into neglect. The lesson of Dona Inês and her lemon trees is not that the law should be ignored, but that a deed and a duty of care can rest in different hands, and that a community is wise to notice the difference before someone with a fence arrives. The courtyard is owned by the company and belongs to the residents, and pretending those are the same thing is how good spaces are lost.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'It-cleft' to assign responsibility
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is she, rather than the absentee company, who has made the space liveable' và 'it was not the law that stopped them but the residents' dùng cấu trúc it-cleft để làm nổi bật tác nhân thực sự, chèn 'rather than' / 'not … but' để loại trừ một ứng viên sai và khẳng định ứng viên đúng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is she, rather than the absentee company, who has made the space liveable' and 'it was not the law that stopped them but the residents' use it-clefts to spotlight the true agent, inserting 'rather than' / 'not … but' to rule out a wrong candidate and assert the right one.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to foreground the space, not the actor
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The fence was quietly abandoned' và 'A courtyard that is owned but never visited is … abandoned' dùng bị động để đặt sự vật (hàng rào, cái sân) lên vị trí chủ ngữ, khi danh tính người thực hiện không quan trọng bằng điều xảy ra với sự vật.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The fence was quietly abandoned' and 'A courtyard that is owned but never visited is … abandoned' use the passive to put the thing (fence, courtyard) in subject position, when who did it matters less than what happened to it.

sampleTranslationVi:
Phía sau một dãy căn hộ ở Lisbon có một cái sân nhỏ, lát những viên gạch nứt nẻ và viền quanh bởi những cây chanh trồng trong chậu. Trên giấy tờ, nó thuộc về một công ty bất động sản có văn phòng cách đó ba quận. Trên thực tế, nó thuộc về bất kỳ ai tình cờ ngồi trên chiếc ghế băng mòn vẹt lúc sáu giờ chiều. Khoảng cách giữa hai sự thật đó chính là nơi câu hỏi thực sự nằm.

Điều mà một cái sân như thế này thực sự thử thách không phải là luật sở hữu mà là sự khác biệt giữa việc nắm giữ giấy tờ và việc thực hành chăm nom. Giấy chứng nhận quyền sở hữu ghi lại ai đã trả tiền; nó chẳng nói gì về việc ai tưới những cây chanh, quét lá sau cơn bão, hay nhắc lũ trẻ tránh xa cái cống vỡ. Trong cái sân này, công việc đó được làm bởi một bà cụ tám mươi tuổi tên là Dona Inês, người chưa bao giờ sở hữu một mét vuông nào của nó. Chính bà ấy, chứ không phải công ty vắng mặt, mới là người làm cho không gian này có thể sống được. Nói rằng bà không có quyền vì tên bà không có trong giấy tờ dường như là nhầm lẫn tờ giấy với chính nơi chốn ấy.

Một ví dụ thứ hai làm rõ thêm điểm này. Khi công ty gần đây đề xuất rào lại một nửa cái sân để cho thuê làm bãi đỗ xe, không phải luật pháp đã ngăn họ lại mà là các cư dân, những người đơn giản là cứ tiếp tục dùng không gian ấy như họ vẫn luôn làm. Các chậu cây không bị dời đi. Những chiếc ghế không bị dọn đi. Hàng rào đã lặng lẽ bị bỏ dở. Điều này cho thấy rằng quyền sở hữu trên thực tế — quyền sở hữu thông qua việc sử dụng và chăm nom hằng ngày — có thể bền vững hơn loại được viết vào hợp đồng.

Công nhận, các cư dân mà công ty của họ nắm giấy tờ có thể lập luận hợp lý rằng tài sản không có sự bảo vệ pháp lý thì chẳng phải là tài sản gì cả. Nếu bất cứ ai nán lại trong một không gian đều có thể đòi quyền với nó, thì không một chủ sở hữu nào còn cảm thấy an toàn, và ít ai sẽ chịu đầu tư để bảo trì bất cứ thứ gì. Phản đối này không phải tầm thường: việc chăm nom chỉ dựa vào thiện chí có xu hướng sụp đổ ngay khi thiện chí cạn kiệt, và phải có ai đó chịu trách nhiệm khi cái cống cuối cùng vỡ ra.

Dẫu vậy, lập luận ủng hộ người dùng hằng ngày không phải là họ nên nắm giấy tờ, mà là quyền sở hữu một không gian chung được hiểu đúng hơn như một mối quan hệ chứ không phải một sự thật. Quyền của công ty là có thật; điều nó thiếu là mối quan hệ. Một cái sân được sở hữu nhưng không bao giờ được lui tới, theo bất kỳ nghĩa nào có ý nghĩa, đều là bị bỏ hoang, trong khi một cái sân được sử dụng, chăm nom và bảo vệ mỗi ngày thì thuộc về — về mặt đạo đức, nếu không phải pháp lý — những người giữ cho nó sống động.

Chính vì các không gian chung kháng cự lại quyền sở hữu đơn lẻ mà chúng thường rơi vào tình trạng bị bỏ bê. Bài học từ Dona Inês và những cây chanh của bà không phải là nên phớt lờ luật pháp, mà là giấy tờ sở hữu và bổn phận chăm nom có thể nằm ở những bàn tay khác nhau, và rằng một cộng đồng khôn ngoan khi nhận ra sự khác biệt đó trước khi ai đó cầm hàng rào tới. Cái sân được công ty sở hữu nhưng thuộc về các cư dân, và giả vờ rằng hai điều đó là một chính là cách mà những không gian tốt bị đánh mất.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to reframe what the courtyard reveals?
options: What a courtyard like this really tests is not the law of property but the difference between holding a title and exercising care.
A courtyard like this is what really tests the property of law and the holding of care.
It tests what a courtyard like this is about property and care exercising.
The courtyard tests the law of property which is what holding a title care exercises.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What … tests is not X but Y' is a what-cleft that delays the key contrast to the end of the clause for emphasis.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with a 'not X but Y' contrast at the end.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to single out the true agent?
options: She made the space liveable and the company did not, which is the absentee one.
It is she, rather than the absentee company, who has made the space liveable.
Making the space liveable is what she did rather than the company absentee.
The absentee company is who she made the space liveable rather than.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is she … who …' is an it-cleft; 'rather than the absentee company' rules out the wrong candidate.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is X … who …' framing one person as the real doer.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive most naturally to foreground the fence?
options: They were abandoning the fence quietly by the residents.
The fence was quietly abandoned.
Quietly the residents abandoning was the fence.
The fence quietly abandons by everyone in the courtyard.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The fence was quietly abandoned' puts the affected thing in subject position when the agent is unimportant.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option starts with 'the fence' and uses 'was + past participle'?

options: Ownership that has been registered with a court
Ownership that exists in practice through use and care, even without legal title
Ownership that only a company can hold
Ownership that is shared equally by everyone in a city
mcQuestions.explanation: 'De facto' means 'in fact' — ownership that holds in practice regardless of what documents say.
mcQuestions.hint: 'De facto' contrasts with 'de jure' (by law). Think 'in fact, not on paper'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces the counter-argument most naturally for B2 writing?
options: Some people are wrong about this and that is the end of it.
Granted, the residents whose company holds the deeds can reasonably claim that property without legal protection is no property at all.
Of course everyone knows the company is just being greedy here.
But the company is bad so its claim does not count at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' opens a concession cleanly and treats the objection as serious rather than dismissing it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option concedes a real point instead of attacking the other side?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly hedges rather than over-claiming?
options: Stewardship that rests on goodwill always collapses instantly and completely.
Stewardship that rests on goodwill alone tends to collapse the moment goodwill runs out.
Stewardship resting goodwill alone collapse the moment runs out goodwill.
Stewardship will absolutely never collapse no matter what happens to goodwill.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tends to collapse' expresses a general pattern, hedging the claim appropriately for argument.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasing softens 'always' into a likely tendency?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'absentee owner' suggest about the company?
options: It owns the courtyard but is not present in its daily life
It has lost its legal claim to the courtyard
It visits the courtyard every single day
It rents the courtyard from the residents
mcQuestions.explanation: An 'absentee' owner holds title but is not physically present or involved.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent formal register?
options: The company totally dropped the ball and the residents were like, no way.
Ownership of a shared space is better understood as a relationship than as a fact.
Basically the courtyard belongs to whoever's hanging out there, end of story.
The whole deeds thing is kind of pointless if nobody actually shows up.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses abstract nouns and a measured comparison; the others slip into casual register.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang like 'dropped the ball', 'like', 'basically', and 'kind of'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best returns to the main argument after the concession?
options: In conclusion, the objection is correct.
Even so, the case for daily users is not that they should hold the deeds, but that ownership of a shared space is better understood as a relationship.
On the contrary, the company has no rights whatsoever.
Anyway, moving on from that point now.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the concession and pivots back to a refined version of the main claim.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the previous point and reasserts the thesis?

options: The legal transfer of property between owners
The careful management and looking-after of something on others' behalf
The act of buying land at auction
The renting out of unused space for profit
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Stewardship' is responsible care of something you may not own.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a steward as someone who looks after, not someone who owns.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a relative clause correctly to add detail about the woman?
options: Dona Inês, who has never owned a square metre of it, waters the lemon trees.
Dona Inês, which has never owned a square metre of it, waters the trees.
Dona Inês, she has never owned a square metre of it, waters the trees.
Dona Inês who never owning a square metre waters the lemon trees.
mcQuestions.explanation: A non-defining relative clause uses 'who' for a person, set off by commas.
mcQuestions.hint: For a person, use 'who', not 'which' or a repeated pronoun.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to lay claim to' mean?
options: To physically lie down on something
To assert a right of ownership or possession over something
To give something away to a community
To repair something that is broken
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Lay claim to' means to declare a right to possess or own.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about 'claiming' a right, not lying down.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: Plant pots were not moved. Chairs were not removed.
The plant pots they didn't move and removing chairs was not done.
Not moving plant pots and chairs were the thing not removed by them.
Plant pots were not moving while the chairs not being removed at all.
mcQuestions.explanation: Two short parallel passives ('were not moved' / 'were not removed') mirror each other for rhetorical rhythm.
mcQuestions.hint: Which pair of sentences shares the same grammatical shape?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, what does the company's claim chiefly lack?
options: A legal document
The relationship of daily use and care
Enough money to maintain the courtyard
Permission from the city government
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay says 'The company's claim is real; what it is missing is the relationship.'
mcQuestions.hint: Reread the fifth paragraph's contrast between claim and relationship.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best expresses the essay's final position without overstating it?
options: The company has no right to the courtyard and should be removed by force.
The courtyard is owned by the company and belongs to the residents, and treating those as the same thing risks losing the space.
Residents always own any space they use, regardless of the law.
Courtyards should simply be sold to whoever uses them most.
mcQuestions.explanation: The conclusion separates legal ownership from belonging and warns against conflating them — a hedged, two-sided close.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the option that holds two ideas (owned vs. belongs) at once.

options: To clean and maintain something
To gradually intrude upon or take over space that is not yours
To pay rent for a shared area
To photograph a public place
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Encroach on' means to advance gradually onto someone else's space or rights.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of a fence slowly creeping into space that belongs to others.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a shared space in your own neighbourhood — a stairwell, a rooftop, a strip of pavement. Who tends it, and who is on the paperwork? If those two people disagreed tomorrow, whose claim would you back, and what would change your mind?
